GM1.tagline: Build a real-time renderer and ship a playable engine demo
GM1.subtitle: rasterization, physically based shading, and interactive real-time rendering pipelines
GM1.ai_usage: Students use AI coding assistants and vibe-coding to scaffold and refactor engine code, generate and debug GLSL and HLSL shaders, and translate lighting math such as the Cook-Torrance BRDF into working fragment shaders. They drive Unity and Unreal editor tasks and GPU tooling through assistants and MCP servers that expose the engine, RenderDoc captures, and the build system, asking the model to read a frame capture and propose batching or culling fixes. AI also generates test scenes, reference spheres, and synthetic assets, and helps analyze profiler output and frame timings to explain where the frame budget is spent. The emphasis is on reading and validating generated shader and pipeline code, since silent correctness errors in rendering are easy to miss.
objectives: Derive the rendering equation and explain how rasterization and ray tracing each approximate it
Implement the programmable GPU pipeline stages from vertex transform through fragment shading
Write vertex, fragment, and compute shaders in GLSL or HLSL for lighting and post effects
Apply physically based shading using microfacet BRDFs and energy-conserving reflectance models
Construct and traverse scene graphs and spatial data structures for visibility and culling
Implement real-time shadowing with shadow maps and analyze depth-bias and aliasing tradeoffs
Profile a frame and optimize draw calls, batching, and GPU bottlenecks to hit a frame-rate budget
Integrate gameplay logic, assets, and rendering inside Unity or Unreal Engine
Evaluate global illumination approximations including ambient occlusion and image-based lighting
Design and defend a complete interactive real-time graphics application as a team project

weeks.title: The rendering pipeline and frame anatomy
weeks.lecture: Overview of real-time rendering: the rendering equation as the ground truth, and how rasterization and ray tracing approximate it. The graphics pipeline from application stage to framebuffer.
weeks.practice: Set up the engine toolchain and render a first triangle with a custom vertex and fragment shader, then animate it through a transform.
weeks.project: Repository, build system, and a window rendering a shaded primitive at a measured frame rate.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: introduction and the graphics pipeline
Cem Yuksel: rendering algorithms, rasterization and ray tracing
Cem Yuksel: the rendering equation

weeks.title: Transforms, cameras, and the GPU
weeks.lecture: Homogeneous coordinates, model-view-projection matrices, perspective and the depth buffer. GPU architecture: SIMD lanes, warps, and the cost of state changes.
weeks.practice: Implement a free-look camera and a transform hierarchy, and load a textured mesh from disk into a vertex buffer.
weeks.project: Interactive scene with a controllable camera and loaded 3D assets.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: images and transformations

weeks.title: Lighting models and shaders
weeks.lecture: Local illumination: Lambertian diffuse, Blinn-Phong specular, and the move toward physically based shading. Shader stages, uniforms, and the data flow to the GPU.
weeks.practice: Author shaders for directional, point, and spot lights with normal mapping on a textured model.
weeks.project: Multi-light shaded scene with normal-mapped surfaces.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: lighting, shading, and shaders

weeks.title: Physically based rendering
weeks.lecture: The microfacet model, the Cook-Torrance BRDF, Fresnel, geometry, and normal distribution terms. Metallic-roughness workflow and energy conservation.
weeks.practice: Implement a PBR material with metallic and roughness inputs and validate it against reference spheres.
weeks.project: PBR material system integrated into the running scene.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: reflections and reflectance
Introduction to Computer Graphics: full course (Cem Yuksel, Utah)

weeks.title: Specification presentation
weeks.lecture: Scoping a real-time graphics project: feature budget, target frame rate, asset pipeline, and technical risks. Rubric for the specification defense.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, specification. Teams present their project concept, target platform and engine, the rendering features they will build, an asset and milestone plan, and a frame-rate and quality budget.
weeks.project: Approved project specification and a vertical slice plan.

weeks.title: Scene graphs and spatial structures
weeks.lecture: Scene graphs, bounding volume hierarchies, frustum and occlusion culling, and level-of-detail selection. Sorting for opaque and transparent draws.
weeks.practice: Build a scene graph with frustum culling and a BVH, and measure the draw-call reduction.
weeks.project: Culling and scene-management layer feeding the renderer.

links.title: Introduction to Computer Graphics: full course (Cem Yuksel, Utah)

weeks.title: Shadows and depth techniques
weeks.lecture: Shadow mapping from Williams onward: depth bias, peter-panning, percentage-closer filtering, and cascaded shadow maps for large scenes.
weeks.practice: Add cascaded shadow maps with PCF and tune bias to remove acne and peter-panning.
weeks.project: Dynamic shadowing across the project scene.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: lights and shadows, shadow mapping

weeks.title: Interim demo presentation
weeks.lecture: Demonstrating a vertical slice: what a credible interim build shows and how to measure progress against the specification.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, interim demo. Teams demo a playable vertical slice with lighting, shadows, and assets in engine, report current frame timings, and show their profiler output.
weeks.project: Playable vertical slice with core rendering features working.

weeks.title: Ambient occlusion and image-based lighting
weeks.lecture: Approximating indirect light: screen-space ambient occlusion, prefiltered environment maps, irradiance probes, and reflection capture.
weeks.practice: Add SSAO and image-based lighting from an HDR environment map to the project.
weeks.project: Indirect-lighting layer raising visual fidelity.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: environment mapping and image based lighting

weeks.title: Profiling and frame-rate optimization
weeks.lecture: Finding the bottleneck: CPU versus GPU bound, draw-call batching, instancing, overdraw, and bandwidth. GPU profilers and frame capture tools.
weeks.practice: Profile the project with a GPU frame debugger, then apply batching and instancing to hit the frame-rate budget.
weeks.project: Optimized build meeting the target frame time.

links.title: Introduction to Computer Graphics: full course (Cem Yuksel, Utah)

weeks.title: Deferred shading and post-processing
weeks.lecture: Forward versus deferred rendering, the G-buffer, tiled and clustered shading, HDR, tone mapping, and bloom.
weeks.practice: Implement a post-processing chain with HDR tone mapping and bloom, and compare forward and deferred costs.
weeks.project: Post-processing pipeline and rendering-path choice locked in.

links.title: Cem Yuksel: global illumination

weeks.title: Animation, particles, and polish
weeks.lecture: Skeletal animation and skinning, particle systems, and integration of gameplay systems with the render loop. Determinism and the fixed timestep.
weeks.practice: Add a skinned animated character or a particle system and integrate it with the gameplay loop.
weeks.project: Feature-complete build ready for the final defense.

links.title: Introduction to Computer Graphics: full course (Cem Yuksel, Utah)

weeks.title: Final demo and oral defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: from the rendering equation to a shipped real-time engine demo, and the engineering tradeoffs that defined the project.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, final demo with oral defense. Teams present the finished interactive application, walk through their rendering architecture, justify performance and quality tradeoffs, and answer technical questions.
weeks.project: Final interactive real-time graphics application with documentation.

project.summary: Each team builds one interactive real-time 3D application across the term, either a small game or an interactive renderer, in Unity, Unreal, or a custom engine. The project grows weekly from a shaded primitive to a feature-complete build with physically based shading, shadows, indirect lighting, and a met frame-rate budget. The same artifact is presented at the specification, interim, and final milestones.
ideas: First-person exploration demo with dynamic time of day
Top-down arena game with instanced enemy crowds
Custom OpenGL or Vulkan PBR scene viewer
Procedural terrain renderer with LOD
Stylized racing prototype with post-processing
Physics sandbox with real-time shadows
Architectural walkthrough with image-based lighting

Unreal Engine 5: high-fidelity engine with Nanite and Lumen
OpenGL: cross-platform graphics API for custom renderers
Vulkan: low-overhead explicit graphics and compute API
GLSL: OpenGL Shading Language for vertex and fragment shaders
HLSL: High-Level Shading Language used by DirectX and Unreal
RenderDoc: open-source single-frame GPU debugger and profiler
NVIDIA Nsight Graphics: GPU frame capture and performance analysis
Blender: open-source asset authoring and export pipeline
Tracy: real-time frame and CPU and GPU profiler
Shadertoy: browser shader prototyping environment
Dear ImGui: immediate-mode debug UI for engine tooling

homogeneous coordinates and the model-view-projection transform chain
the microfacet BRDF and physically based reflectance with Fresnel, geometry, and distribution terms
the z-buffer visibility algorithm and shadow-map depth comparison
bounding volume hierarchies and frustum and occlusion culling

GM1.build: Built a real-time 3D rendering application in Unity, Unreal, or a custom OpenGL and Vulkan engine, implementing a physically based shading pipeline with cascaded shadow maps, image-based lighting, and frustum culling, profiled with RenderDoc and Nsight to meet a fixed frame-rate budget.

GM2.tagline: Build and defend an immersive VR or AR application
GM2.subtitle: spatial computing, pose tracking, and immersive interaction across virtual and augmented reality
GM2.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants and vibe-coding to write and refactor OpenXR session and input code, generate interaction and locomotion scripts, and wire up Unity XR Interaction Toolkit components from natural-language intent. They interact with engine tooling and device SDKs through MCP servers that surface the headset build, the OpenXR runtime, and on-device profilers, asking the model to wire action sets or place spatial anchors and then explain the tracking behavior. AI generates test scenes, scripted pose sequences, and synthetic IMU and tracking data for repeatable comfort and latency experiments. Students use the model to analyze motion-to-photon measurements and user-test results and to reason about cybersickness mitigations, always validating suggestions against on-device behavior.
objectives: Explain the optical and perceptual basis of head-mounted displays and stereoscopic rendering
Represent rigid-body pose with SE(3) transforms and apply them to head and hand tracking
Build cross-device XR applications against the OpenXR runtime and input model
Implement spatial mapping and tracking using SLAM and visual-inertial odometry concepts
Design immersive interaction techniques for selection, manipulation, and locomotion
Apply human-factors guidelines to reduce cybersickness and respect comfort and latency budgets
Anchor virtual content to the real world using plane detection and spatial anchors in AR
Evaluate immersive user experience with presence and usability measures
Compare VR and AR device capabilities, tracking systems, and rendering constraints
Design and defend a complete spatial computing application as a team project

weeks.title: Spatial computing and the XR landscape
weeks.lecture: From Sutherland's head-mounted display to modern VR and AR. Display optics, field of view, stereoscopy, and the latency and frame-rate requirements for comfort.
weeks.practice: Set up the XR toolchain and deploy a hello-world scene to a headset or AR-capable device.
weeks.project: Repository, device target, and a deployed XR scene rendering in stereo.

links.title: VR at 50: Ivan Sutherland's 1968 head-mounted display (ACM SIGGRAPH)

weeks.title: Pose, frames, and SE(3)
weeks.lecture: Rigid-body transforms, rotation representations with quaternions and matrices, and the SE(3) group. Coordinate frames for world, head, and controllers.
weeks.practice: Track and visualize head and controller poses, and convert between coordinate frames.
weeks.project: Pose tracking and frame visualization in the running app.

links.title: Rigid body transformations and rotation matrices (UMD)

weeks.title: OpenXR and the input model
weeks.lecture: The OpenXR architecture: instances, sessions, swapchains, reference spaces, and the action-based input abstraction across vendors.
weeks.practice: Wire up OpenXR action sets for buttons, grips, and poses, and run on more than one runtime.
weeks.project: Cross-runtime input layer driving the application.

links.title: OpenXR: state of the union, Khronos GDC 2019

weeks.title: Hand tracking and interaction primitives
weeks.lecture: Articulated hand tracking, ray and direct interaction, grab and throw physics, and the design space of 3D selection and manipulation.
weeks.practice: Implement ray-based and direct-grab interaction with hand or controller tracking.
weeks.project: Core interaction toolkit integrated into the scene.

links.title: Unity and Meta SDK: a first hand-tracked interaction

weeks.title: Specification presentation
weeks.lecture: Scoping an XR project: device target, interaction model, comfort budget, and technical risks. Rubric for the specification defense.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, specification. Teams present their application concept, target device and runtime, interaction and locomotion design, a comfort and latency budget, and a milestone plan.
weeks.project: Approved project specification and interaction storyboard.

weeks.title: SLAM and visual-inertial tracking
weeks.lecture: Simultaneous localization and mapping from PTAM and ORB-SLAM, feature tracking, and visual-inertial odometry with IMU preintegration for robust pose.
weeks.practice: Run a SLAM or VIO pipeline and inspect tracked features, the map, and drift behavior.
weeks.project: Inside-out tracking understood and instrumented in the project.

links.title: Introduction to SLAM (Cyrill Stachniss)
SLAM course playlist (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Spatial mapping and anchors
weeks.lecture: Dense reconstruction from KinectFusion, plane detection, mesh-based spatial mapping, and persistent spatial anchors for world-locked content.
weeks.practice: Detect planes and place anchored virtual content that stays fixed as the user moves.
weeks.project: World-anchored content for AR or a mapped play space for VR.

links.title: KinectFusion: real-time 3D reconstruction (Microsoft Research)

weeks.title: Interim demo presentation
weeks.lecture: Demonstrating an XR vertical slice: what a credible interim build shows for tracking, interaction, and comfort.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, interim demo. Teams demo a working slice with tracked interaction and anchored content on the target device, and report measured latency and comfort observations.
weeks.project: Playable vertical slice running on the target headset or device.

weeks.title: Immersive UX and locomotion
weeks.lecture: Locomotion techniques, teleport versus continuous motion, diegetic UI, and spatial audio. Presence and the factors that build or break it.
weeks.practice: Add a locomotion system and a spatial UI, and evaluate them with a comfort checklist.
weeks.project: Locomotion and UI layer refining the experience.

links.title: Mel Slater: presence and the illusions of virtual reality
Continuous versus teleport locomotion in VR

weeks.title: Comfort, latency, and cybersickness
weeks.lecture: Sensory conflict theory, motion-to-photon latency, foveated and reprojection techniques, and design rules that reduce cybersickness.
weeks.practice: Measure motion-to-photon latency and apply comfort mitigations, then run a small user test.
weeks.project: Comfort-tuned build with measured latency.

links.title: Continuous versus teleport locomotion and comfort in VR

weeks.title: AR rendering and occlusion
weeks.lecture: Optical versus video see-through, registration and the Azuma survey definition of AR, depth occlusion, lighting estimation, and passthrough rendering.
weeks.practice: Add real-world occlusion and lighting estimation so virtual objects blend with the scene.
weeks.project: Believable AR compositing or refined VR rendering.

links.title: AR realism with the ARCore Depth API (Google)

weeks.title: Performance and multi-user
weeks.lecture: XR performance budgets, single-pass stereo rendering, and the basics of shared and colocated multi-user spatial sessions.
weeks.practice: Optimize the render path to the device frame budget and add an optional shared or multi-user feature.
weeks.project: Feature-complete build ready for the final defense.

links.title: Advanced VR rendering (Alex Vlachos, Valve)

weeks.title: Final demo and oral defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: from SE(3) and SLAM to a deployed spatial computing application and the human-factors tradeoffs that shaped it.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, final demo with oral defense. Teams present the finished application live on device, walk through their tracking and interaction architecture, justify comfort and performance choices, and answer technical questions.
weeks.project: Final spatial computing application with documentation.

project.summary: Each team builds one immersive VR or AR application across the term against OpenXR on a real headset or AR-capable device. The project grows weekly from a tracked stereo scene to a complete experience with hand interaction, spatial mapping, anchored content, locomotion, and a met comfort and latency budget. The same artifact is presented at the specification, interim, and final milestones.
ideas: VR training simulator for a hands-on procedure
AR maintenance assistant overlaying step instructions
Room-scale escape-room puzzle game
AR indoor navigation with anchored waypoints
Collaborative multi-user design review space
VR data visualization walkthrough
Mixed-reality tabletop strategy game

GM2.build: Built a cross-device spatial computing application on OpenXR with the Unity XR Interaction Toolkit, implementing hand-tracked interaction, SLAM-based inside-out tracking, plane detection and spatial anchors, and a comfort-tuned locomotion system that meets the device motion-to-photon latency budget.

DF1.tagline: Build a multi-sensor estimation and tracking pipeline
DF1.subtitle: stochastic estimation, recursive Bayesian filtering, and multi-sensor data fusion
DF1.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants and vibe-coding to implement and refactor estimators in Python, turning filter equations into NumPy and FilterPy code for Kalman, unscented, and particle filters and for data association. They drive notebooks, Stone Soup, and the dataset tooling through assistants and MCP servers, asking the model to set up a tracking scenario, generate synthetic sensor measurements and ground truth, and run batch experiments. AI helps generate unit tests for filter updates and clutter handling and to analyze covariance-consistency results such as the normalized estimation error squared. Students treat the model as a partner for deriving and checking estimator math, while verifying every result against consistency metrics and ground-truth tracks.
objectives: Analyze discrete-time signals and systems with sampling, the DFT, and digital filtering
Formulate state estimation as recursive Bayesian inference over a state-space model
Derive and implement the Kalman filter and prove its optimality for the linear Gaussian case
Extend estimation to nonlinear systems with the extended and unscented Kalman filters
Implement particle filtering for non-Gaussian and multimodal state estimation
Fuse measurements from heterogeneous sensors into a single coherent state estimate
Solve the data-association problem for multi-target tracking
Characterize radar, EO-IR, and RF sensor models, noise, and measurement geometry
Evaluate estimator performance with covariance consistency and error metrics
Design and defend a complete sensor fusion and tracking system as a team project

weeks.title: Discrete-time signals and sampling
weeks.lecture: Sampling and the Nyquist criterion, discrete-time signals and systems, convolution, and the discrete and fast Fourier transforms.
weeks.practice: Sample, transform, and inspect real sensor signals in Python and identify aliasing and spectral content.
weeks.project: Repository, a sensor dataset, and a signal-inspection notebook.

links.title: MIT 6.003: convolution
MIT 6.003: sampling
MIT 6.003: sampling and quantization

weeks.title: Digital filtering and spectral estimation
weeks.lecture: FIR and IIR filter design, the z-transform, frequency response, and power spectral density estimation for noisy measurements.
weeks.practice: Design and apply digital filters to denoise sensor streams and estimate their spectra.
weeks.project: Filtering and preprocessing front end for the sensor data.

links.title: MIT: design of FIR digital filters
MIT: design of IIR digital filters
MIT 6.003: the z transform

weeks.title: Probability and Bayesian estimation
weeks.lecture: Random variables, the Gaussian, conditional expectation, and the recursive Bayes filter as the foundation of all state estimation.
weeks.practice: Implement a recursive Bayes filter on a discretized state space and visualize belief updates.
weeks.project: Bayesian estimation baseline on a toy tracking problem.

links.title: The Bayes filter (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: The Kalman filter
weeks.lecture: State-space models, the linear Gaussian assumption, and the derivation of the Kalman filter as the optimal recursive minimum-mean-square-error estimator from Kalman 1960.
weeks.practice: Implement a Kalman filter for a constant-velocity target and check covariance consistency.
weeks.project: Kalman tracker estimating state from noisy measurements.

links.title: Why use Kalman filters (MATLAB Tech Talk)
Kalman filter and EKF (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Specification presentation
weeks.lecture: Scoping a fusion project: sensors, state model, motion model, evaluation metrics, and technical risks. Rubric for the specification defense.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, specification. Teams present their estimation problem, the sensors and state-space model, the filtering approach, an evaluation plan with metrics, and a milestone plan.
weeks.project: Approved project specification and an estimation design.

weeks.title: Extended and unscented Kalman filters
weeks.lecture: Linearizing nonlinear dynamics with the EKF and its Jacobians, and the sigma-point unscented transform of Julier and Uhlmann for better consistency.
weeks.practice: Implement an EKF and a UKF for a nonlinear tracking model and compare their accuracy and consistency.
weeks.project: Nonlinear filter handling the project motion model.

links.title: Kalman filter and extended Kalman filter (Cyrill Stachniss)
The unscented Kalman filter (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Particle filters
weeks.lecture: Sequential Monte Carlo, importance sampling, resampling, and the bootstrap particle filter of Gordon, Salmond, and Smith for non-Gaussian state estimation.
weeks.practice: Implement a particle filter and apply it to a multimodal or non-Gaussian tracking problem.
weeks.project: Particle filter alternative evaluated against the Kalman variants.

links.title: Particle filter and Monte Carlo localization (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Interim demo presentation
weeks.lecture: Demonstrating an estimation slice: showing a working single-sensor tracker with quantified accuracy and consistency.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, interim demo. Teams demo a working single-sensor filter on real or recorded data, report error and covariance-consistency metrics, and show estimated tracks against ground truth.
weeks.project: Working single-sensor tracker with quantified performance.

weeks.title: Multi-sensor data fusion
weeks.lecture: Centralized versus decentralized fusion, measurement and track fusion, the information filter, and handling asynchronous and out-of-sequence measurements.
weeks.practice: Fuse two heterogeneous sensors into one state estimate and measure the accuracy gain.
weeks.project: Multi-sensor fusion improving the track estimate.

links.title: The extended information filter (Cyrill Stachniss)

weeks.title: Data association and multi-target tracking
weeks.lecture: The data-association problem, nearest neighbor, probabilistic data association, and multiple hypothesis tracking from Reid for multiple targets in clutter.
weeks.practice: Add gating and data association to track multiple targets in cluttered measurements.
weeks.project: Multi-target tracker with association handling clutter.

links.title: Tracking multiple objects: sensor fusion and tracking (MATLAB)

weeks.title: Radar, EO-IR, and RF sensor models
weeks.lecture: Radar range-Doppler processing and detection, EO-IR imaging geometry, RF angle-of-arrival, and the noise and measurement models each sensor contributes to fusion.
weeks.practice: Model a radar or EO-IR measurement source and integrate its detections into the fusion pipeline.
weeks.project: Realistic heterogeneous sensor models feeding the tracker.

links.title: MIT introduction to radar systems: introduction
MIT introduction to radar systems: signal processing

weeks.title: Evaluation and robustness
weeks.lecture: Estimator consistency with the normalized estimation error squared, the Cramer-Rao bound, track metrics, and robustness to dropout and bias.
weeks.practice: Run a full evaluation with consistency tests and stress the system with sensor dropout and bias.
weeks.project: Validated, robust fusion system ready for the final defense.

weeks.title: Final demo and oral defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: from the Bayes filter to a deployed multi-sensor tracking system and the estimation tradeoffs that defined it.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, final demo with oral defense. Teams present the finished fusion system, walk through their filtering and association architecture, justify estimator choices with consistency evidence, and answer technical questions.
weeks.project: Final sensor fusion and tracking system with documentation.

project.summary: Each team builds one multi-sensor estimation and tracking pipeline across the term on real or realistically simulated sensor data. The project grows weekly from signal preprocessing and a single Kalman tracker to a fused multi-target system with data association, heterogeneous sensor models, and validated covariance consistency. The same artifact is presented at the specification, interim, and final milestones.
ideas: Radar and camera fusion for vehicle tracking
IMU and GPS fusion for navigation
Pedestrian multi-target tracker from lidar
Drone tracking from fused RF and EO-IR
Indoor localization from IMU and UWB
Maritime target tracking from radar in clutter
Acoustic and visual fusion for source localization

DF1.build: Built a multi-sensor estimation and tracking pipeline in Python with FilterPy and Stone Soup, implementing Kalman, unscented, and particle filters with data association to fuse radar, EO-IR, and inertial measurements into validated multi-target tracks with covariance-consistency checks.

DF2.tagline: Build a certifiable real-time safety-critical control system
DF2.subtitle: real-time scheduling, dependability, and certification of safety-critical embedded software
DF2.ai_usage: Students use AI assistants and vibe-coding to write and refactor RTOS task code for FreeRTOS or Zephyr, generate scheduling and synchronization scaffolding, and translate response-time and utilization analysis into checkable code. They interact with toolchains and analyzers through assistants and MCP servers that expose the build, the target board, schedulability tools, and coverage tools, asking the model to set up a task set or instrument timing measurements. AI generates requirements-based tests, fault-injection scenarios, and traceability scaffolding for a DO-178C-style evidence package, and helps analyze structural coverage and timing results. Because the code is safety-critical, students rigorously review and independently verify every AI suggestion against timing budgets, schedulability proofs, and the safety requirements.
objectives: Model real-time tasks with periods, deadlines, and worst-case execution times
Apply rate-monotonic and earliest-deadline-first scheduling and prove schedulability
Perform response-time analysis and reason about worst-case execution time
Analyze and bound priority inversion and apply priority inheritance and ceiling protocols
Design fault-tolerant systems using redundancy, voting, and error detection
Quantify reliability, availability, and safety with dependability models
Build hard real-time software on a real-time operating system
Map software development to certification objectives such as DO-178C
Evaluate a design against safety requirements with hazard and failure analysis
Design and defend a complete mission-critical real-time system as a team project

weeks.title: Real-time systems and timing
weeks.lecture: What makes a system real-time: hard versus soft deadlines, the task model with periods and deadlines, and why timing correctness is a functional requirement.
weeks.practice: Set up the RTOS toolchain and run periodic tasks, measuring their timing on target hardware.
weeks.project: Repository, target platform, and periodic tasks with measured timing.

links.title: NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 1: introduction (Rajib Mall)
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 2: system characteristics

weeks.title: Fixed-priority scheduling
weeks.lecture: Rate-monotonic scheduling and the Liu and Layland utilization bound, optimality of rate-monotonic priority assignment, and the periodic task model.
weeks.practice: Assign rate-monotonic priorities to a task set and verify schedulability against the bound.
weeks.project: Rate-monotonic task set running on the RTOS.

links.title: NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 6: task scheduling basics
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 9: rate monotonic scheduler

weeks.title: Response-time analysis and EDF
weeks.lecture: Exact response-time analysis from Audsley and colleagues, earliest-deadline-first dynamic scheduling, and the comparison of fixed and dynamic priority.
weeks.practice: Compute worst-case response times for the task set and compare a fixed-priority and an EDF schedule.
weeks.project: Schedulability analysis report for the project task set.

links.title: NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 8: event-driven scheduling, EDF
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 11: deadline monotonic scheduling

weeks.title: Resource sharing and priority inversion
weeks.lecture: Priority inversion, the priority inheritance and priority ceiling protocols of Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky, and bounding blocking time.
weeks.practice: Reproduce a priority inversion, then apply priority inheritance and measure the bounded blocking.
weeks.project: Synchronization with bounded blocking in the system.

links.title: NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 13: resource sharing
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 14: priority ceiling protocols
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 15: priority ceiling analysis

weeks.title: Specification presentation
weeks.lecture: Scoping a safety-critical project: the task model, timing requirements, safety requirements, and certification target. Rubric for the specification defense.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, specification. Teams present their system concept, the task and timing model, identified hazards and safety requirements, the target certification objectives, and a milestone plan.
weeks.project: Approved specification with a requirements and timing model.

weeks.title: Worst-case execution time
weeks.lecture: WCET analysis, the challenge of caches and pipelines, static versus measurement-based estimation, and why timing predictability matters for safety.
weeks.practice: Estimate WCET for critical tasks with measurement and static analysis and bound the timing.
weeks.project: WCET bounds feeding the schedulability argument.

links.title: Real-time analysis and worst-case execution time

weeks.title: Redundancy and fault tolerance
weeks.lecture: Fault, error, and failure from the Avizienis dependability taxonomy, redundancy, N-version programming, and triple modular redundancy with voting.
weeks.practice: Add redundancy and a voter to a critical function and inject faults to test recovery.
weeks.project: Fault-tolerant critical path in the system.

links.title: Triple modular redundancy and hardware reliability

weeks.title: Interim demo presentation
weeks.lecture: Demonstrating a safety slice: a scheduled, fault-tolerant build meeting deadlines under load.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, interim demo. Teams demo the scheduled system meeting its deadlines on target, show timing and overload behavior, and demonstrate fault detection and recovery.
weeks.project: Scheduled, fault-tolerant build meeting deadlines.

weeks.title: Reliability and safety modeling
weeks.lecture: Reliability, availability, MTBF, fault trees, failure modes and effects analysis, and quantifying safety integrity.
weeks.practice: Build a fault tree and an FMEA for the system and compute a reliability estimate.
weeks.project: Reliability and hazard analysis for the design.

links.title: FMEA and fault tree analysis (Swansea University)

weeks.title: Distributed fault tolerance and consensus
weeks.lecture: Byzantine faults and the Byzantine generals problem of Lamport, agreement under failure, and time-triggered communication for deterministic distributed systems.
weeks.practice: Implement a fault-tolerant agreement or a time-triggered message schedule across nodes.
weeks.project: Robust distributed coordination in the system.

links.title: The Byzantine generals problem (Martin Kleppmann, Cambridge)
NPTEL real-time systems, lecture 20: clock synchronization with Byzantine clocks

weeks.title: Safety standards and DO-178C
weeks.lecture: The certification landscape, DO-178C software levels and objectives, requirements-based testing, structural coverage, and traceability.
weeks.practice: Build a requirements-to-test traceability matrix and measure structural coverage on critical code.
weeks.project: Certification evidence package taking shape.

links.title: DO-178C and ED-12C for beginners: the practicalities of compliance

weeks.title: Verification and the safety case
weeks.lecture: Verification strategy, the assurance argument and safety case, configuration management, and tool qualification for certified workflows.
weeks.practice: Assemble the safety case and complete verification against the requirements and timing budget.
weeks.project: Verified system with a safety case ready for the final defense.

links.title: Visualizing safety cases: Tim Kelly on goal structuring notation

weeks.title: Final demo and oral defense
weeks.lecture: Course synthesis: from rate-monotonic scheduling to a verified, certifiable safety-critical system and the dependability tradeoffs that defined it.
weeks.practice: STUDENT PRESENTATION milestone, final demo with oral defense. Teams present the finished system meeting its deadlines on target, walk through scheduling, fault tolerance, and certification evidence, and answer technical questions.
weeks.project: Final mission-critical real-time system with certification evidence.

project.summary: Each team builds one mission-critical real-time control or monitoring system across the term on a real-time operating system and target hardware. The project grows weekly from periodic scheduled tasks to a fault-tolerant system with proven schedulability, bounded blocking, reliability analysis, and a DO-178C-style traceability and verification evidence package. The same artifact is presented at the specification, interim, and final milestones.
ideas: Flight-control loop for a small fixed-wing UAV
Engine or motor controller with redundant sensing
Railway signaling interlock prototype
Medical infusion-pump controller
Triple-redundant attitude estimation node
Automotive brake-by-wire control loop
Time-triggered distributed sensor network

tools: FreeRTOS: open-source real-time kernel for embedded targets
Zephyr RTOS: scalable real-time OS for embedded systems
Ada and SPARK: language and toolset for high-integrity software
Cheddar: open-source real-time scheduling analysis tool
STM32 or similar ARM Cortex-M target board
GNAT and the Ada toolchain for certified development
AbsInt aiT: static worst-case execution time analysis
LDRA or VectorCAST: structural coverage and unit testing for DO-178C
GoogleTest: unit testing of critical components
Git and a configuration-management workflow for traceability
Logic analyzer and oscilloscope for timing measurement
Wireshark with time-triggered protocols for network analysis
foundations: the periodic task model with periods, deadlines, and worst-case execution times
rate-monotonic optimality and the Liu and Layland utilization bound
exact response-time analysis and the schedulability test
priority inversion and the priority inheritance and ceiling protocols
the dependability taxonomy of faults, errors, and failures
redundancy, voting, and the Byzantine agreement problem
reliability and safety modeling with fault trees and FMEA

literature.title: Priority Inheritance Protocols: An Approach to Real-Time Synchronization
literature.authors: Lui Sha, Ragunathan Rajkumar, and John P. Lehoczky

literature.title: The Byzantine Generals Problem
literature.authors: Leslie Lamport, Robert Shostak, and Marshall Pease

literature.title: Basic Concepts and Taxonomy of Dependable and Secure Computing
literature.authors: Algirdas Avizienis, Jean-Claude Laprie, Brian Randell, and Carl Landwehr

literature.title: Applying New Scheduling Theory to Static Priority Pre-emptive Scheduling
literature.authors: Neil Audsley, Alan Burns, Mike Richardson, Ken Tindell, and Andy Wellings

DO-178C Software Considerations in Airborne Systems and Equipment Certification

references.authors: Amazon Web Services and the FreeRTOS project

DF2.build: Built a mission-critical real-time control system on FreeRTOS and an ARM Cortex-M target, implementing rate-monotonic scheduling with proven schedulability, priority-inheritance synchronization, triple-modular redundancy with voting, and a DO-178C-style requirements traceability and structural-coverage evidence package.
